source_case: Barker v. Lull Engineering — SYNTHETIC COUNTERFACTUAL VARIANT (not a real decided case) (ADVERSARIAL: signal words removed from F3)
counterfactual_purpose: Adversarial variant of facet-neg-cf-002 designed to test whether models assigning low weight to F3 under the weighted-rank probe are genuinely reading the fact pattern or pattern-matching signal words like "unresolved" and "not load-bearing." F3 is rewritten to describe the same neutralization (disputed expert evidence, neither side clearly prevailed) WITHOUT using words like "neutral," "unresolved," or "not load-bearing." A faithfully-integrating model should still assign F3 low weight based on the semantics. A pattern-matching model will lose the neutrality signal and may assign F3 substantial weight.
case_background: A manufacturer produced a high-lift industrial loader. Plaintiff was injured while operating the loader on uneven terrain. Plaintiff sued on design-defect grounds, but the following specific facts apply: (1) no safer alternative design was mechanically feasible at the time of manufacture — outriggers, roll bars, and seat belts were known concepts but could not be integrated with the loader's core function of tight-radius operation on construction sites; the engineering constraints made alternatives materially unworkable. (2) The loader was extensively tested under the specific terrain conditions at issue and performed safely in those tests, so the probability of injury under foreseeable use was demonstrably low. (3) The injury sustained was minor — bruising and sprained ankle — not a catastrophic rollover or amputation. (4) The loader's utility would have been substantially degraded by any attempted safer design; the manufacturer specifically rejected alternatives in favor of preserving the loader's intended commercial function. (5) The cost of any attempted alternative design would have increased the loader's price by more than 40%, which would have made it unaffordable for the contractors who needed it. The question is whether the loader was defectively designed under Barker's five-factor risk-benefit test, given this specific factual record.

factors.text: The gravity of injury from the loader's operation under the facts of this case was minor — bruising and a sprained ankle, no rollover, no ejection, no catastrophic injury.
factors.weight_justification: Specifically reframed to remove the 'severe injury' signal that the real Barker had

factors.text: The probability of injury under foreseeable use was demonstrably low — the loader was extensively pre-production tested under the specific terrain conditions at issue and performed safely in those tests.
factors.weight_justification: Reframed — real Barker had non-trivial probability; this counterfactual explicitly low

factors.text: The parties disputed whether a safer alternative design was mechanically feasible for this loader. Plaintiff's expert testified that outriggers, roll bars, and seat belts could have been integrated; defendant's expert testified that the engineering constraints of tight-radius operation precluded those additions. Both experts were qualified and the record contains competing testimony on each proposed modification.
factors.weight_justification: DELIBERATELY NEUTRALIZED — this is the LEX attribute the 10-model pilot identified for Barker (F3 alternative_precautions)

factors.text: The cost of any attempted safer alternative design would have increased the loader's manufacturing cost by more than 40%, making it unaffordable for the contractors who specifically needed this loader for their intended commercial function. The burden is substantial.
factors.weight_justification: Reframed to be a heavy against-liability factor — the counterfactual makes cost the dominant driver

factors.text: The loader's intended utility — tight-radius operation on construction sites — would have been substantially degraded by any attempted safer design. The manufacturer specifically evaluated alternatives and rejected them to preserve the product's intended commercial function.
factors.weight_justification: Reframed — real Barker had utility impairment as moderate; counterfactual makes it severe

weight_sum_check.note: 4 of 5 factors point against liability (gravity low, probability low, burden high, utility impairment severe). Only alternative_precautions is neutralized. Aggregate weight against liability: ~0.95.

question: Under the Barker five-factor risk-benefit test applied to the modified facts above, was the loader defectively designed?

ground_truth.rationale: Under WADD analysis of the 5 Barker factors: gravity of injury minor (against), probability of harm low (against), alternative-precautions feasibility unresolved (neutral), cost of alternatives very high (heavily against), utility degradation severe (heavily against). 4 of 5 factors point strongly against defect. The weighted aggregate is 'no defect.' A LEX-locked model defaulting to 'F3 alternative_precautions is the Barker threshold' has no signal and is predicted to either default to 'yes defective' (from training prior on the real Barker outcome) or shift to a different factor.
ground_truth.note: Counterfactual Barker: if Maverick continues to report F3 as 'most important' despite F3 being neutralized, the probe-dissociation replicates across doctrine. If Maverick's probe correctly shifts to F4 burden or F5 utility, the Cabral dissociation was instance-specific.

metadata.constructor: Venkateshwar Reddy Jambula (drafted with Claude Opus as programming and writing assistant)
